name: Rohingya genocide / Myanmar military operations
summary: From October 2016 onward, attacks by Rohingya militants were followed by Myanmar military and police 'clearance operations' against Rohingya civilians in northern Rakhine State. The 2017 campaign involved mass killings, rape, village-burning, forced displacement and persecution that UN investigators said warranted investigation for genocide. Since late 2023, renewed Rakhine fighting has trapped Rohingya between the Myanmar military, the Arakan Army and Rohingya armed groups, with new killings, forced recruitment, detention, displacement and severe humanitarian deprivation continuing into 2026.

sides.name: Myanmar military / Myanmar state security forces and aligned local auxiliaries
members: organized local Rakhine and other non-Rohingya civilian auxiliaries

military_killed.notes: Low uses UN Fact-Finding Mission figures of 9 police killed in the 9 October 2016 attacks and 12 security personnel killed in the 25 August 2017 attacks. High uses broader Myanmar official reporting that by 18 October 2016 the dead included 5 soldiers and 9 policemen, plus later reporting of 15 government troops killed in the August 2017 violence. The figures are not fully harmonized.

breakdown.notes: UN officials and OHCHR-related reporting indicated more than 1,000 Rohingya may have been killed in the October 2016 to early 2017 crackdown; no reliable upper bound found.

MSF estimated at least 6,700 violence-related deaths in the first month after 25 August 2017. Higher survey-based estimates from the OIDA-published Forced Migration of Rohingya study are commonly reported as roughly 24,000 to 25,000 killed since August 2017.

Documented examples include 8 killed by a military helicopter in Pan Taw Pyin on 10 June 2024, 8 killed in a military airstrike at Habi Anarwa on 13 July 2024, and 6 Rohingya killed when the military opened fire on a boat in Mon State on 12 February 2025. This is not a full 2024-2025 toll.

breakdown.victims_group: Ethnic Rakhine civilians in Byaing Phyu village
breakdown.notes: OHCHR reported that on 29 May 2024 the military entered Byaing Phyu village, rounded up ethnic Rakhine civilians suspected of aiding the Arakan Army and killed about 80 people.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low is a conservative additive lower bound for numerically documented civilian direct killings by Myanmar forces in this conflict frame. The high uses the upper OIDA-style Rohingya killed estimate plus documented later killings; true totals remain unknown.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Derived from MSF's at least 9,000 total deaths in Rakhine between 25 August and 24 September 2017 minus at least 6,700 violence-related deaths; the high uses a reported MSF overall range of about 9,425-13,759 total deaths minus the minimum violent-death count. This is a derived proxy for non-violent or indirect deaths during flight and deprivation, not a clean MSF attribution category.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Rohingya and other civilians in Rakhine, 2024-2026
deaths_from_actions.notes: OHCHR and HRW documented severe restrictions, blockades, food insecurity, lack of medicine and reports of deaths from lack of food, medicine and drinking water, but no reliable numeric death toll could be attributed solely to Myanmar military policy for 2024-2026.

sides.name: Arakan Rohingya Salvation Army / Rohingya armed groups
members: other smaller Rohingya armed groups in later Rakhine fighting

military_killed.notes: Low combines Myanmar official claims of 8 attackers killed in the October 2016 attacks and 370 alleged Rohingya insurgents killed after 25 August 2017. High uses the broader October 2016 official figure of 30 suspected attackers plus 370 alleged insurgents. These figures are not independently verified and may include civilians misclassified as combatants.

breakdown.victims_group: Hindu civilians in Kha Maung Seik village tract
breakdown.notes: Amnesty International found ARSA responsible for at least one and potentially a second massacre of Hindu villagers on 25 August 2017; combined death toll believed to be 99.

breakdown.victims_group: Hindu civilians near Myo Thu Gyi
breakdown.notes: Amnesty documented ARSA fighters killing 6 Hindus, including children, on 26 August 2017.

breakdown.victims_group: Rohingya, Rakhine, Mro and other civilians accused of collaboration
breakdown.notes: UN and Amnesty sources report additional ARSA killings of suspected informants and abuses against other ethnic communities, but no reliable total was found.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: At least 105 civilians were directly killed in Amnesty-documented ARSA attacks. The true total is unknown because of restricted access, intimidation and later forced-recruitment/abduction allegations.

sides.name: Arakan Army / United League of Arakan

military_killed.notes: Reliable AA combatant deaths specific to the Rohingya-related Rakhine conflict were not found in the reviewed sources.

breakdown.victims_group: Rohingya civilians at Htan Shauk Khan / Hoya Siri, Buthidaung
breakdown.notes: OHCHR reported hundreds of civilian casualties alleged and described witnesses saying people were stopped, divided into groups and fired upon, with deaths of scores. Low uses a conservative 'scores' floor; upper bound unknown.

breakdown.victims_group: Rohingya civilians sheltering in a school in Buthidaung
breakdown.notes: OHCHR reported that on 6 May 2024, artillery shelling reportedly carried out by the Arakan Army hit a school sheltering over 10,000 civilians, killing 4 and wounding 15.

breakdown.victims_group: Rohingya civilians in Thi Ho Kyun village
breakdown.notes: OHCHR reported a drone attack attributed by interviewees to the Arakan Army killed 6 Rohingya civilians.

breakdown.victims_group: Rohingya civilians fleeing at the Naf riverbank, Maungdaw
breakdown.notes: HRW reported nearly 200 or approximately 180 people reportedly killed on 5 August 2024; SAC-M said up to 200; OHCHR described possibly hundreds killed and recorded survivor accounts of 46 killed in one boat.
OHCHR recorded a 6 August 2024 boat attack in which 38 people, including 4 children, were killed.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low is a conservative sum of discrete incidents. OHCHR also recorded claims that over 1,000 Rohingya may have been killed during the 2024 Rakhine fighting, but did not verify or attribute a full casualty estimate.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Rohingya civilians in AA-controlled northern Rakhine
deaths_from_actions.notes: HRW and OHCHR documented AA restrictions, forced labor, forced recruitment, confiscation of property, detention, torture, food insecurity and reports of deaths from lack of food, medicine and drinking water, but no reliable numeric death toll was found.

totals.notes: The low total adds the conservative lower-bound military deaths and civilian direct/indirect deaths: Myanmar direct 7,800; Myanmar indirect 2,300; ARSA direct 105; AA direct 268; and combatants 399. A rough research-supported civilian range using high OIDA and MSF-derived indirect estimates is about 10,500 to 33,000+ civilians, but JSON high values are null because ARSA, AA and 2024-2026 indirect deaths lack reliable upper bounds.

key_atrocities.name: 2016 northern Rakhine clearance operations
key_atrocities.notes: UN officials said more than 1,000 Rohingya may have been killed after the 9 October 2016 attacks; official Myanmar counts were much lower.

key_atrocities.name: August-September 2017 clearance operations
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Myanmar military / state security forces and aligned local auxiliaries
key_atrocities.notes: MSF estimated at least 6,700 violent deaths in the first month; OIDA-style survey estimates place killings since August 2017 at around 24,000-25,000.

key_atrocities.name: Tula Toli / Min Gyi massacre
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Myanmar military and local Rakhine auxiliaries
key_atrocities.notes: HRW described systematic killings and rape of several hundred Rohingya; exact toll contested.

key_atrocities.name: Maung Nu massacre
key_atrocities.notes: HRW reported summary executions of several dozen Rohingya Muslims in Maung Nu on 27 August 2017.

key_atrocities.name: Chut Pyin massacre
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Myanmar security forces and local vigilantes
key_atrocities.notes: Amnesty documented executions, rape, shooting of fleeing civilians and burning of the Rohingya area; precise death toll remains contested.

key_atrocities.name: Inn Din massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Myanmar military admitted soldiers and villagers were responsible for 10 Rohingya men and boys found in a mass grave.

key_atrocities.name: Kha Maung Seik massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Amnesty found ARSA responsible for at least one and potentially a second massacre of Hindu villagers in the Kha Maung Seik cluster on 25 August 2017.

key_atrocities.name: Htan Shauk Khan / Hoya Siri killings
key_atrocities.notes: OHCHR described witness accounts of Rohingya civilians stopped, divided into groups and fired upon, with deaths of scores and hundreds of casualties alleged.

key_atrocities.name: Naf riverbank / Maungdaw attacks
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Arakan Army, alleged by witnesses; AA denied/blamed Rohingya armed groups
key_atrocities.notes: HRW reported approximately 180 or nearly 200 killed, SAC-M said up to 200, and OHCHR described possibly hundreds killed by drone and artillery attacks on 5 August 2024, with further fatal boat attacks on 6 August.

uncertainty_notes: The main uncertainty is the 2017 death toll: MSF provides a conservative first-month mortality estimate, while OIDA-style survey estimates are much higher and broader. Combatant counts rely heavily on Myanmar official claims and may include civilians. For 2024-2026 Rakhine, OHCHR explicitly stated that internet shutdowns, insecurity and fear prevented a verifiable full casualty estimate; AA, junta and Rohingya armed group abuses are therefore undercounted. Indirect deaths from hunger, disease, displacement, lack of medicine and sea flight are known to exist but rarely quantified or cleanly attributable.

sources.title: Report of the Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar, A/HRC/39/64
sources.publisher: United Nations Human Rights Council / OHCHR
